benchmark_details.name: GLUE-X
benchmark_details.overview: A unified benchmark named GLUE-X for evaluating out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness in Natural Language Understanding models. GLUE-X expands GLUE by including 15 publicly available and newly collected OOD test datasets, covering eight standard GLUE tasks, and provides unified cross-domain evaluations over 21 pre-trained language models. It focuses on domain generalization where a model trained on a source domain is evaluated on target domains without any labeled or unlabeled data from the target domains.
benchmark_details.data_type: text (classification datasets: sentiment analysis, natural language inference, textual entailment, paraphrase identification, textual similarity, linguistic acceptability, question-answering instances converted to classification)

purpose_and_intended_users.goal: Provide an extension of GLUE with the same in-domain training data but multiple out-of-distribution (OOD) test sets to enable fair, unified evaluation of cross-domain generalization (single-source domain generalization) for Natural Language Understanding models.
purpose_and_intended_users.limitations: Primary focus is on the OOD robustness of text classification tasks. GLUE-X currently does not include language generation tasks such as machine translation, summarization, and dialogue. Extending GLUE-X to more real-world datasets from different domains is listed as future work.

data.source: GLUE-X uses the same in-domain training data as GLUE and constructs OOD test sets from 15 datasets, including public datasets and newly collected/re-constructed datasets. Explicitly mentioned OOD datasets include: IMDB, Yelp, Amazon, Flipkart, Grammar Test (CoLA-OOD), SICK, MNLI-mismatched, SNLI, NewsQA (reconstructed for QNLI), RTE, SciTail, HANS, MRPC, QQP, and Twitter. (See paper Table 1 and Appendix A for dataset selection and curation details.)
data.size: Per-dataset sizes reported in paper Table 1: IMDB 50,000 examples; Yelp 598,000 examples; Amazon 4,000,000 examples; Flipkart 205,041 examples; Grammar Test (CoLA-OOD) 304,277 examples; SICK 9,840 examples; MNLI-mismatched 9,832 examples; SNLI 570,152 examples; NewsQA (reconstructed) 119,525 examples; SciTail 26,527 examples; HANS 60,000 examples; Paraphrase combined (MRPC/QQP) reported 404,276 examples; Twitter 16,777 examples. GLUE-X contains 15 OOD datasets in total.
data.annotation: Human annotation via a crowdsourcing company: for human performance, 1,000 testing samples were used per OOD dataset; multiple labelers annotated the same data point (typically two annotators per instance; ten annotators for SICK). Inter-Annotator Agreement (IAA) average over the 15 OOD datasets is 0.857. Rationale marking: two labelers annotated sampled instances for sentiment tasks (IMDB, Yelp, Amazon) with 6,000 sampled instances per dataset; IAA (F1) for rationale marking on IMDB, Yelp, Amazon are 0.874, 0.871, and 0.840 respectively.

methods: Automated metrics following GLUE task-specific metrics
Human evaluation (crowdsourced annotators for OOD test samples and rationale annotation)
Robustness ranking by decreased ratio between OOD and ID performance
Post-hoc feature analysis: rationale overlap using contextual decomposition / sensitivity of contextual decomposition
metrics: Task-specific GLUE metrics (e.g., Accuracy; Matthew's Correlation for CoLA; STS-B similarity scores as used in GLUE)
Average across metrics for tasks with multiple metrics (per GLUE)
Average across task scores to obtain overall OOD performance
Decreased ratio between OOD and ID performance (percentage drop)
Rationale overlap measured with Precision, Recall, F1
methodology.calculation: For tasks with multiple metrics, first average the task metrics. The overall OOD performance is the average of all task scores (as in GLUE/SuperGLUE). Robustness ranking uses the decreased ratio from ID to OOD performance and Friedman rank computed as rankF = (1/n) * sum_i rank_i where n is number of tasks.
methodology.interpretation: Higher averaged OOD scores indicate better out-of-distribution generalization. Lower decreased ratio (ID→OOD) indicates better robustness. Friedman rank is used to compare models across multiple tasks. Rationale overlap F1 between model-selected rationales and human rationales is used as a proxy for trust/interpretability.
methodology.baseline_results: Baseline results reported in the paper include: ELECTRA-large achieved best reported GLUE-X average OOD 74.62% and ID 89.18% (Table 3). Human baseline average OOD reported as 80.14% (Table 4). The paper reports OOD performance for 21 pre-trained models and compares decreased ratios and Friedman ranks (see Table 3 and Table 4).
methodology.validation: Hyper-parameters selected by grid search on ID validation sets; best-performing checkpoint on ID used for OOD testing. Human annotation quality controlled with IAA thresholds per task (average IAA 0.857). For human baselines, 1,000 sampled test instances per OOD dataset were used.

ethical_and_legal_considerations.privacy_and_anonymity: The paper states that public available datasets were used to establish the GLUE-X leaderboard and that no private data was used.
ethical_and_legal_considerations.data_licensing: The code and data are open-sourced under the CC-BY-NC-SA license.
